one_line_takeaway: A domain-adapted MPT-7B fine-tuned on a medical-device maker's historical CVE evaluations automates VEX categorization, CVSS environmental scoring, and analyst comments at ~200x human speed with 0.93 F1 on affected/not-affected triage, showing DAPT + instruction diversity — not dataset size — drives industrial vulnerability-assessment LLMs.
key_finding: CVE-LLM substantially outperforms same-size and RAG baselines on this task and roughly matches its test-set performance in live deployment. On the 44K test set it reaches micro-F1 0.93 VEXCategory, 0.89 VEXJustification, 0.95 Vector, and ROUGE-L 0.80 Internal / 0.88 Customer comments — versus Mistral-7B (0.64 / 0.60 / 0.59 / 0.71 / 0.70), Llama2-7B (0.56 / 0.53 / 0.57 / 0.62 / 0.64), and a much weaker Llama3-70B RAG setup (0.25 / 0.12 / 0.13 / 0.19 / 0.28). Two ablation lessons stand out: (1) domain-adaptive pretraining + vocabulary expansion is the biggest lever — DAPT+SFT beats SFT-only MPT-7B on every field (e.g., VEXCategory 0.93 vs 0.83, Vector 0.95 vs 0.84); (2) diversity of instruction types matters more than raw dataset size, and consolidating all outputs into one instruction (CI) or omitting a stop delimiter (WD, VEXCategory collapses to 0.66) sharply degrades quality. Temperature and nucleus sampling had NO observed effect (the specialized data makes the top token dominant); beam size >7 hurts. Operationally, model inference is ~1s/evaluation vs an expert average of 229s over 500 evaluations (~200x faster), with vLLM giving a 10x serving speedup and adaptive batching cutting mean latency from 8s to 4.5s.
core_contribution: CVE-LLM is a fine-tuned large language model (built on Mosaic ML's MPT-7B) that automatically produces vulnerability EVALUATIONS for a single medical-device manufacturer's (Siemens Healthineers') product portfolio. Unlike prior work that mostly does vulnerability DETECTION from source code, this paper's own contribution is generating device-specific impact assessments purely from asset descriptions plus vulnerability notification text. Given an (asset, notification) pair, the model outputs five structured artifacts: VEXCategory (Affected / NotAffected), VEXJustification (why NotAffected, one of ~6 categories), the CVSS environmental Vector, an Internal Comment (mitigation guidance), and a Customer Comment (customer-facing summary). The three claimed contributions are: (1) best-practice recipe for training a vulnerability LM in an industrial context (DAPT + SFT, vocabulary expansion, delimiter/template design), (2) a benchmark comparison of LLMs on this vulnerability-assessment task, and (3) a human-in-the-loop framework that prioritizes 'Affected' evaluations for expert review to speed up triage.
threat_model: The paper addresses defensive vulnerability management, not an adversarial ML threat model. The operational problem: medical device manufacturers (MDMs) are FDA-required to monitor all 3rd-party software components in their devices and evaluate each emerging CVE against every product the component appears in, over device lifetimes up to 20 years. Manual evaluation does not scale against thousands of new vulnerabilities per month, causing dangerous triage delays for a sector the paper cites as the most breached (809 compromises, 56M victims in 2023). The system's goal is to automate the high-volume 'not applicable' determinations (the largest efficiency gain) while routing genuinely 'Affected' cases to human experts. The paper does NOT analyze adversarial robustness of the LLM itself (e.g., prompt injection, poisoned notifications) — its human-in-the-loop design is a quality/safety control, not an adversarial defense.
limitations: (1) Single-vendor, single-portfolio study on proprietary Siemens Healthineers data — the datasets are not public and results may not transfer to other MDMs. (2) Performance degrades on completely novel asset/notification types, confirming the model interpolates from historically similar evaluations rather than reasoning about unseen designs; the authors explicitly note new-to-product CVEs still need first-time human investigation because design information is absent from the LLM. (3) Long instructions (multiple chained vulnerabilities, ~10% of the test set) underperform despite MPT's long-context capability. (4) Free-text comments exhibit factual errors — omitted or falsely inserted affected/fixed software versions and component names — which is safety-relevant for medical devices. (5) Underrepresented CVSS-vector classes have low F1 despite high aggregate Vector F1. (6) Only zero-shot inference was tested (no few-shot / chain-of-thought). (7) Production VEXCategory F1 drops to 0.83 (from 0.93 on test) on genuinely new data, so the human-in-the-loop reviewer remains essential. (8) No adversarial-robustness evaluation of the model itself.

datasets_used: DAPT corpus: 320K vulnerability documents (218K public NVD CVE docs + 102K organization-internal vulnerability docs); 288K train / 32K val
Instruction-tuning dataset: 440K instructions from the Evaluations data, split 352K train / 44K val / 44K test (80:10:10)
Underlying organizational data at time of writing: ~1.6K assets, 134K notifications (11K unique used in evaluations), 174K evaluations, 152K unique components
CVE-LLM-Prod live evaluation: ~2K fresh expert evaluations over new notifications/assets across a 2-month deployment
Expert-time baseline measured over 500 human evaluations
quantitative_results: CVE-LLM-Eval test-set micro-F1: VEXCategory 0.93, VEXJustification 0.89, Vector 0.95 (ROUGE-L: Internal Comment 0.80, Customer Comment 0.88)
CVE-LLM-Eval* (end-to-end with rule-based post-processing): VEXJustification 0.92, Vector 0.97, Customer Comment 0.89
CVE-LLM-Prod (live, new data): VEXCategory 0.83, VEXJustification 0.95, Vector 0.97, Internal 0.70, Customer 0.75
DAPT effect (Table 6): CVE-LLM-Base vs SFT-only MPT-7B — VEXCategory 0.93 vs 0.83, VEXJustification 0.89 vs 0.80, Internal 0.80 vs 0.64, Customer 0.88 vs 0.69, Vector 0.95 vs 0.84
Without-delimiter ablation (WD) collapses scores: VEXCategory 0.66, VEXJustification 0.51, Vector 0.70
Inference speed: ~1s/eval (model) vs 229s average human expert (~200x); vLLM 10x serving speedup; adaptive batching 8s to 4.5s mean latency
Temperature (0-1) and nucleus sampling (top-p 0-1) produced no output change; beam size >7 degrades all fields
baselines_compared: Llama3-70B with RAG over the test set (weakest: VEXCategory 0.25, VEXJustification 0.12, Vector 0.13)
Mistral-7B trained with same DAPT+SFT (VEXCategory 0.64, Vector 0.59)
Llama2-7B trained with same DAPT+SFT (VEXCategory 0.56, Vector 0.57)
SFT-only MPT-7B (no domain adaptation) as an internal ablation baseline
Human security-expert evaluation as the operational/time baseline
